memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Poetry" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: "Accursed Who Brings to Light of Day"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Accursed Who Brings to Light of Day"

memes.label: Aengus in Yeats
memes.description: Aengus Óg — the Irish god of love, dreams, and birds — as a recurring symbolic figure in W. B. Yeats's poetry, representing the visionary power of love and the dream-state that mediates between mortal and immortal worlds.

memes.label: Ancestral Houses
tags: Meditations in Time of Civil War

memes.label: Anne Yeats
memes.description: Anne Butler Yeats (1919–2001), the daughter of W. B. Yeats and George Hyde-Lees, was the subject of several of her father's most personal late poems and later became a distinguished Irish painter and stage designer in her own right.

memes.label: Art and Creativity in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring Yeats's ideas about poetry-making, artistic creation, and the role of imagery in the creative process.

memes.label: Artifice of Eternity
memes.description: Yeats's symbol of the perfected work of art that transcends mortal decay — the golden bird of "Sailing to Byzantium" that sings of "what is past, or passing, or to come."

memes.label: "Baile and Aillinn"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Baile and Aillinn"

memes.label: Childhood and Youth in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring Yeats's early life — his Sligo childhood, his family, and the formative experiences that shaped the young poet's sensibility.

memes.label: "Colophon"
memes.description: "Front matter — Colophon"

memes.label: Concepts Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all concept pages in the Yeats poetry wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Connacht and the West of Ireland
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages about the western Irish landscapes — Connacht, Galway, Sligo, Knocknarea — that shaped Yeats's imagination from childhood to his final resting place at Drumcliff.

memes.label: Constance Markievicz
memes.description: Constance Markievicz (1868–1927), born Constance Gore-Booth of Lissadell, was an Irish revolutionary, suffragist, and the first woman elected to the British House of Commons — a figure whose transformation from Anglo-Irish aristocrat to republican militant embodied the revolutionary currents that shaped Yeats's late poetry.

memes.label: Crazy Jane
memes.description: Crazy Jane is the most powerful of W. B. Yeats's late poetic personae — an earthy, sexually frank, theologically defiant old woman who gave voice to the anti-self of the aging poet and is the central figure of the "Words for Music Perhaps" sequence (1932).

memes.label: "Crossways"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Crossways"

memes.label: Custom and Ceremony
memes.description: Yeats's valuation of ceremony, custom, and inherited social forms as the frameworks that give life order, beauty, and meaning.

memes.label: Death and Mortality in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring death, mortality, the afterlife, and elegy in W. B. Yeats's poetry and philosophy.

memes.label: Edmund Burke
memes.description: Edmund Burke (1729–1797) — Irish-born statesman and political philosopher, celebrated by W. B. Yeats in "The Seven Sages" as a foundational figure of the Anglo-Irish intellectual tradition.

memes.label: "Endnotes"
memes.description: "Front matter — Endnotes"

relations.label: The Countess Cathleen and Various Legends and Lyrics

memes.label: Events Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all event pages in the Yeats poetry wiki — historical events, literary movements, and biographical milestones that shaped Yeats's work.

memes.label: Featured Content
memes.description: A dynamic showcase of featured and premium-quality pages in the Yeats poetry wiki, ranked by reference count.

memes.label: Fenian Cycle in Yeats
memes.description: The Fenian Cycle of Irish mythology — the tales of Finn mac Cumhaill, Oisin, and the Fianna — as a source of heroic imagery, elegiac vision, and nationalist symbolism in W. B. Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: "From The Green Helmet and Other Poems"
memes.description: "Poem(s): From The Green Helmet and Other Poems"

memes.label: County Galway
memes.description: County Galway in the west of Ireland — home to Coole Park, Thoor Ballylee, and the landscape of Yeats's mature poetry, including "The Wild Swans at Coole" and "Meditations in Time of Civil War".

relations.label: Meditations in Time of Civil War

memes.label: Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn
memes.description: The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, founded in 1888, was the most influential Western occult organisation of the late nineteenth century — the initiatic brotherhood to which W. B. Yeats belonged for over two decades and which provided the symbolic vocabulary for his mature poetry and philosophical system.
aliases: Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn

memes.label: The Great Memory
memes.description: The Great Memory — also called the Anima Mundi, Spiritus Mundi, or world-soul — is the Yeatsian concept of a universal storehouse of images, symbols, and memories accessible to the poetic imagination, drawn from Neoplatonic philosophy and occult tradition.

memes.label: Heroes and Warriors in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages about the heroic figures, mythic warriors, and martial ideals that populate Yeats's poetry — from Cuchulain to the Fenian heroes.

memes.label: "How Ferencz Renyi Kept Silent"
memes.description: "Poem(s): How Ferencz Renyi Kept Silent"

memes.label: "Imprint"
memes.description: "Front matter — Imprint"

memes.label: "In Church"
memes.description: "Poem(s): In Church"

memes.label: "In a Drawing-Room"
memes.description: "Poem(s): In a Drawing-Room"

memes.label: "In the Firelight"
memes.description: "Poem(s): In the Firelight"

memes.label: "In the Seven Woods"
memes.description: "Poem(s): In the Seven Woods"

memes.label: 'Introductory Poems from "The Shadowy Waters"'
memes.description: 'Poem(s): Introductory Poems from "The Shadowy Waters"'

memes.label: Irish Civil War
memes.description: The Irish Civil War (1922–1923), fought between supporters and opponents of the Anglo-Irish Treaty, profoundly shaped Yeats's later poetry — particularly "Meditations in Time of Civil War" and "Nineteen Hundred and Nineteen" — and forced the aging poet to confront the violence lurking beneath the ideals of Irish nationalism.

memes.label: Irish History and Politics in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring the Irish political events, nationalist movements, and historical figures that shaped Yeats's public life and civic poetry.

memes.label: Irish Mythology
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages on Irish mythological figures, cycles, and themes that appear in Yeats's poetry — from the Tuatha Dé Danann to Cúchulainn and the Sidhe.

memes.label: Irish War of Independence
memes.description: The Irish War of Independence (1919–1921) — the guerrilla conflict that followed the Easter Rising and led to the Anglo-Irish Treaty — is the political crucible in which Yeats's greatest poems were written, shaping the poet's late meditations on violence, sacrifice, and the birth of a nation.

relations.label: Meditations in Time of Civil War

memes.label: Jonathan Swift
memes.description: Jonathan Swift (1667–1745), the Anglo-Irish satirist and author of Gulliver's Travels, was for W. B. Yeats one of the foundational figures of the Irish intellectual tradition — a model of fierce independence, savage wit, and the refusal to serve any master but the truth of his own vision.

memes.label: "Kanva on Himself"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Kanva on Himself"

memes.label: Knocknarea in Yeats
memes.description: Knocknarea — the flat-topped mountain above Sligo Bay, crowned with Queen Maeve's cairn — as a symbol of the mythic landscape, the threshold of the Otherworld, and the "country of the young" in W. B. Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: "Landmarks"
memes.description: "Back matter — Landmarks"

memes.label: "Life"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Life"

memes.label: "Love Song"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Love Song"

memes.label: "Love and Death"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Love and Death"

memes.label: Love and Relationships in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring love, desire, and the key personal relationships — especially with Maud Gonne — that shaped Yeats's life and poetry.

memes.label: "Michael Robartes and the Dancer"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Michael Robartes and the Dancer"

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: A dynamically ranked list of the top 50 most-referenced wiki pages, ordered by total backlink count.

memes.label: Mysticism and Occult
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages covering the mystical and occult traditions that shaped Yeats's thought — from the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn to Neoplatonic mysticism and the Spiritus Mundi.

memes.label: Nature and Landscape in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages tagged with "nature" or "landscape" — the Irish countryside, elemental imagery, and natural settings in Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: Niamh in Yeats
memes.description: Niamh — the fairy queen of Tír na nÓg who carries Oisin away from the mortal world — as a symbol of the Otherworld's seductive call, immortal beauty, and the cost of visionary transport in W. B. Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: Nobility in Yeats
memes.description: Yeats's ideal of nobility — not merely social rank but spiritual and aesthetic greatness expressed through inherited culture, courtesy, and the ceremonial life.

memes.label: Oliver Goldsmith
memes.description: Oliver Goldsmith (1728–1774) — Irish-born novelist, poet, and dramatist, celebrated by W. B. Yeats in "The Seven Sages" as a foundational figure of the Anglo-Irish literary tradition.

memes.label: "On Mr. Nettleship's Picture at the Royal Hibernian Academy"
memes.description: "Poem(s): On Mr. Nettleship's Picture at the Royal Hibernian Academy"

memes.label: Organizations and Institutions
memes.description: A dynamic index of organizations and institutions relevant to W. B. Yeats — theatres, mystical societies, political groups, and cultural institutions.

memes.label: Persons Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all person pages — historical figures, mythological characters, and Yeats's contemporaries referenced in his poetry.

memes.label: Philosophy in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages tagged with "philosophy" — the philosophical traditions, ideas, and thinkers that shaped Yeats's intellectual framework.

memes.label: Places Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all place pages — real Irish locations and symbolic landscapes in Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: "She Who Dwelt Among the Sycamores"
memes.description: "Poem(s): She Who Dwelt Among the Sycamores"

memes.label: County Sligo
memes.description: County Sligo in the west of Ireland — the landscape of W. B. Yeats's childhood and the setting for many of his most famous poems, from "The Lake Isle of Innisfree" to "Under Ben Bulben".

relations.label: Alternative Song for the Severed Head in 'The King of the Great Clock Tower'

memes.label: Sligo in Yeats
memes.description: County Sligo — "Yeats Country" — as the spiritual homeland, imaginative seedbed, and recurrent landscape symbol in W. B. Yeats's poetry, rooted in childhood summers and charged with mythological resonance.

memes.label: "Song of the Faeries"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Song of the Faeries"

memes.label: Saint Patrick
memes.description: Saint Patrick, the fifth-century Christian missionary to Ireland, appears in W. B. Yeats's early epic "The Wanderings of Oisin" as the antagonist of the pagan hero — the voice of Christian orthodoxy in dialogue with the older, enchanted Ireland of the Fenian cycles.

memes.label: "Street Dancers"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Street Dancers"

memes.label: Supernatural and Otherworldly in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of pages exploring the supernatural, otherworldly, and visionary dimensions of W. B. Yeats's poetry — fairies, spirits, dreams, and the Celtic Otherworld.

memes.label: Symbolism in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages tagged with "symbolism" — recurring images, motifs, and symbolic frameworks in Yeats's poetry and philosophy.

memes.label: The Sidhe
memes.description: The Sidhe (Aes Sidhe) — the fairy folk of Irish mythology, the ancient gods of Ireland who retreated into the hills and forts — as a central supernatural presence in W. B. Yeats's poetry and folklore.

memes.label: "The Tower"
memes.description: "Poem(s): The Tower"

memes.label: "The Two Titans"
memes.description: "Poem(s): The Two Titans"

memes.label: "The Wild Swans at Coole"

memes.label: "The Wind Among the Reeds"
memes.description: "Poem(s): The Wind Among the Reeds"

memes.label: "Time and the Witch Vivien"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Time and the Witch Vivien"

memes.label: Tír na nÓg
memes.description: The Land of Youth in Irish mythology — the Otherworld realm to which the fairy woman Niamh carries Oisin in Yeats's narrative poem "The Wanderings of Usheen".

memes.label: "To Some I Have Talked with by the Fire"
memes.description: "Poem(s): To Some I Have Talked with by the Fire"

memes.label: Tradition and Ceremony in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of pages exploring tradition, ceremony, custom, and the aristocratic ideal in W. B. Yeats's poetry and social philosophy.

memes.label: Tragic Joy
memes.description: Tragic joy — the affirmation of life in the face of its greatest suffering and loss — is the emotional keynote of W. B. Yeats's late poetry, a quality he identified with the highest artistic achievement and the deepest spiritual wisdom.

memes.label: Tree of Life in Yeats
memes.description: The Tree of Life as a central symbol in Yeats's poetry, appearing as the hazel tree, the axletree of heaven, and the cosmic axis connecting worlds.

memes.label: "Uncopyright"
memes.description: "Back matter — Uncopyright"

memes.label: Unity of Being
memes.description: Yeats's ideal of the complete integration of all human faculties — body, mind, and spirit — into a harmonious whole, most famously expressed in "Among School Children."

memes.label: "When You Are Sad"
memes.description: "Poem(s): When You Are Sad"

memes.label: "Where My Books Go"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Where My Books Go"

memes.label: "Wisdom and Dreams"
memes.description: "Poem(s): Wisdom and Dreams"

memes.label: Wisdom and Knowledge in Yeats
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages about the philosophical and esoteric traditions of knowledge — Neoplatonism, the anti-self, Plotinus, wisdom — that shaped Yeats's understanding of what it means to know.

memes.label: Works Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all work pages — poetry collections, plays, and prose works by W. B. Yeats.

memes.label: Yeatsian Symbolism
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring the symbolic imagery in Yeats's poetry — the rose, tower, swan, gyre, and other recurring symbols that form his private mythology.

memes.label: A Vision
memes.description: A Vision (1925, revised 1937) is W. B. Yeats's major prose work of occult philosophy — an elaborate symbolic system of historical cycles, lunar phases, and the soul's journey, derived from the automatic writing of his wife George Yeats and presented through the fictional personae of Michael Robartes and Owen Aherne.

memes.label: Abbey Theatre
memes.description: The Abbey Theatre, co-founded by W. B. Yeats and Lady Gregory in 1904, was the institutional home of the Irish Literary Revival — the stage upon which the Irish dramatic movement was built and the centre of Yeats's public life in Dublin.

memes.label: Aengus
memes.description: Aengus (Angus Óg), the Celtic god of love, youth, and poetic inspiration, appears throughout Yeats's poetry as the dreamer-god whose visions sustain the imaginative life — 'where Aengus dreams, from sun to sun.'

memes.label: Ancestral Houses
memes.description: Ancestral Houses is Section I of W. B. Yeats's Meditations in Time of Civil War (1928), opening the sequence with a meditation on whether beauty — the rich man's flowering lawns and great houses of the Anglo-Irish aristocracy — depends on violence and inherited wealth.

relations.label: Meditations in Time of Civil War

memes.label: Angels and Spirits in Yeats
memes.description: Yeats's treatment of angels, spirits, and ghosts as intermediaries between the visible and invisible worlds, rooted in Hermetic and Celtic traditions.

memes.label: Apocalypse in Yeats
memes.description: Apocalyptic and millenarian imagery in the poetry of W. B. Yeats, centred on "The Second Coming" and the cyclical theory of history in A Vision.

memes.label: Aristocracy in Yeats
memes.description: Yeats's idealisation of the Anglo-Irish aristocracy as embodying ceremony, courtesy, and tradition — a defence against modern democratic leveling.

relations.label: The Friends That Have It I Do Wrong
Meditations in Time of Civil War

memes.label: Automatic Writing
memes.description: Automatic writing — the practice of writing without conscious control, believed to be dictated by spirit communicators — was the medium through which Yeats's wife George Hyde-Lees produced the material that became A Vision (1925), the occult-philosophical system underlying Yeats's mature poetry.

relations.label: Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn

memes.label: Beast Imagery in Yeats
memes.description: Animal and bestial imagery in Yeats' poetry, from the apocalyptic "rough beast" of "The Second Coming" to the demon-and-beast symbolism of "Michael Robartes and the Dancer."

memes.label: Ben Bulben in Yeats
memes.description: Ben Bulben, the flat-topped mountain visible from Sligo, is the defining landscape of Yeats's imaginative geography — the mountain under which he chose to be buried and whose "back" he claimed to have climbed in his final poetic self-epitaph.

memes.label: Ben Bulben
memes.description: Ben Bulben, the distinctive flat-topped mountain in Co. Sligo, is the site of Yeats's burial at Drumcliff churchyard and the commanding landscape presence of his final poem, "Under Ben Bulben."

memes.label: Bird Imagery in Yeats
memes.description: The rich system of bird imagery in Yeats' poetry — swans, hawks, doves, and eagles as vehicles for meditations on the soul, desire, eternity, and the violence of history.

memes.label: Byzantium
memes.description: Yeats's symbol of the eternal city of art — an imagined Byzantium where religious, aesthetic, and practical life were one, represented in the paired poems "Sailing to Byzantium" and "Byzantium" as the destination of the soul's journey away from natural decay toward the artifice of eternity.

memes.label: Byzantium
memes.description: Byzantium in Yeats's poetry is not the historical Constantinople but an imagined city of pure artifice — a symbolic place where the soul can escape the cycle of generation and achieve the permanence of art, "the artifice of eternity."

memes.label: Celtic Mythology
memes.description: The body of Celtic mythological narratives and figures that shaped Yeats's poetic imagination and furnished his symbolic vocabulary.

memes.label: The Celtic Twilight
memes.description: The Celtic Twilight (1893, expanded 1902) is W. B. Yeats's prose collection of Irish folklore stories and supernatural encounters — a cornerstone of his early mythological period.

memes.label: Ceremony of Innocence
memes.description: The Yeatsian concept of the "ceremony of innocence" — the ritual and customary forms that sustain civilization, and their drowning in the violence of history, most famously articulated in "The Second Coming."

memes.label: Connemara
memes.description: Connemara, the western region of County Galway, appears in Yeats's poetry through its distinctive grey skies and rough-woven cloth — images of the austere beauty of the Irish west.

memes.label: Coole Park
memes.description: Coole Park, Lady Augusta Gregory's estate in Co. Galway, was the creative center of the Irish Literary Revival — a place of refuge and inspiration for Yeats, where he wrote some of his most significant poetry and where the "autumn beauty" of the swans became a lasting symbol of time and loss.

memes.label: Cuchulain
memes.description: Cuchulain (Cú Chulainn), the greatest hero of the Ulster Cycle, appears in Yeats's poetry and plays as the archetype of heroic sacrifice — particularly as the dying hero tied to a pillar, an image that haunted Yeats's imagination.

memes.label: Dance in Yeats
memes.description: "Dance as a central symbol in Yeats's poetry, representing the unity of body and soul, art and life, and the ecstatic transcendence of opposition — culminating in the famous question from 'Among School Children': 'How can we know the dancer from the dance?'"

memes.label: Death in Yeats
memes.description: Death in Yeats's poetry operates as a transformative threshold rather than an endpoint — a passage between worlds that connects the poet to Irish folklore, mystical experience, and heroic tradition.

memes.label: Deirdre
memes.description: Deirdre (1907) is W. B. Yeats's one-act tragedy based on the ancient Irish legend of Deirdre of the Sorrows — first performed at the Abbey Theatre during the Irish Literary Revival.

memes.label: Dream and Vision
memes.description: In Yeats's poetry, dream and vision function as portals to the supernatural — a mode of perception that bypasses rationality to access mythological truth, ancestral memory, and poetic inspiration.

memes.label: Druidism and Celtic Mysticism
memes.description: The Druidic and Celtic mystical tradition in W. B. Yeats's poetry — ancient Irish wisdom, Druid dreams, and the occult revival of pre-Christian spiritual knowledge.

memes.label: Dublin
memes.description: Dublin is the capital of Ireland and the theatre of W. B. Yeats's public life — the city of the Abbey Theatre, the Easter Rising, the Municipal Gallery controversy, and the intellectual ferment of the Irish Literary Revival that Yeats helped to lead.

memes.label: Eternal Beauty
memes.description: Yeats's concept of eternal, unchanging beauty as a Platonic ideal that transcends time, decay, and mortality — distinct from mortal or physical beauty that "passes like a dream."

memes.label: Fairy and Faery Lore
memes.description: The fairy world in W. B. Yeats's poetry and folklore — the Sidhe, faery doctors, and the Otherworld as a symbol of imagination, immortal longing, and the Irish peasant spiritual tradition.

memes.label: Fergus mac Róich
memes.description: Fergus mac Róich, the legendary king of Ulster who gave up his throne, appears in Yeats's poetry as a figure of renunciation and the wisdom that comes from abandoning power — a counter-type to the heroic Cuchulain.

memes.label: Fire and Passion
memes.description: Fire in Yeats's poetry is the element of spiritual passion, poetic inspiration, and purgatorial transformation — the "holy fire" that burns away the dross of the merely personal to reveal the essential self.

memes.label: County Galway
memes.description: County Galway in the west of Ireland — home to Coole Park, Thoor Ballylee, and the landscape of Yeats's mature poetry, including "The Wild Swans at Coole" and "Meditations in Time of Civil War".

relations.label: Meditations in Time of Civil War

memes.label: Gyre
memes.description: The gyre is W. B. Yeats's central symbolic concept — a spiralling cone that represents the cyclical movement of history, the soul, and all human experience, most famously in "The Second Coming" — "Turning and turning in the widening gyre"

memes.label: Gyres
memes.description: Yeats's geometrical symbol of historical cycles — interpenetrating cones representing the rise and fall of civilisations and the soul's progression.

memes.label: Hazel Tree in Yeats
memes.description: The hazel tree as a recurring symbol in Yeats's poetry — associated with wisdom, poetic inspiration, the Celtic Tree of Life, and Otherworld encounters through its mythological connection to the Well of Wisdom and the Salmon of Knowledge.

memes.label: Heroic Imagery in Yeats
memes.description: The use of heroic figures — Cuchulain, Fergus, Oisin — and the ideal of tragic heroism in W. B. Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: Hill of Tara
memes.description: The Hill of Tara in Co. Meath, the ancient seat of the High Kings of Ireland, appears in Yeats's poetry as a symbol of lost Irish sovereignty and the mythological unity that preceded colonial fragmentation.

memes.label: Horse Imagery in Yeats
memes.description: The horse and rider as recurring images in Yeats' poetry — mythological steeds, Fenian horsemen, and the bridle as a figure for artistic and philosophical control.

memes.label: Immortality in Yeats
memes.description: Yeats's evolving conception of immortality — from Celtic Otherworld to Hermetic transmigration to the artifice of eternity — in his poetry.

memes.label: Innisfree
memes.description: The lake-island of Innisfree in Yeats' poetry, a pastoral ideal of solitude and spiritual peace rooted in his Sligo childhood.

memes.label: Innisfree
memes.description: A small uninhabited island in Lough Gill, County Sligo — the setting of Yeats's most famous early poem "The Lake Isle of Innisfree" and an enduring symbol of pastoral escape from urban modernity into a life of natural simplicity and poetic solitude.

memes.label: Ireland
memes.description: Ireland is the central geographical, political, and imaginative locus of W. B. Yeats's entire body of work — the nation whose mythology he revived, whose politics he wrestled with, and whose "terrible beauty" he celebrated and mourned in poems that span half a century.

memes.label: Irish Folklore
memes.description: Irish folklore — the oral traditions, legends, and folk beliefs of rural Ireland — provided W. B. Yeats with his primary poetic subject matter and shaped his conviction that the Irish peasantry preserved, in degraded form, an ancient visionary wisdom.

memes.label: Irish Mythology
memes.description: Irish mythology is the foundational imaginative resource of W. B. Yeats's poetry — the body of pre-Christian legend from which he drew characters, symbols, and a vision of Ireland's spiritual identity, synthesising the Ulster Cycle, Fenian Cycle, and Mythological Cycle with occult and symbolist poetics.

memes.label: Irish Nationalism
memes.description: Irish nationalism — the political and cultural movement for Irish independence from British rule — was a shaping force in W. B. Yeats's life and poetry, providing his central personal relationships, his public role, and a body of symbols through which he negotiated the tension between art and politics.

memes.label: John Aherne
memes.description: John Aherne (also Owen Aherne), one of W. B. Yeats's invented personae, appears alongside Michael Robartes in the philosophical poems of *The Tower* and *The Winding Stair* as a figure through whom Yeats explored his "philosophy of life and death."

memes.label: John MacBride
memes.description: Major John MacBride (1868–1916), the man who married Maud Gonne in 1903 and was executed after the Easter Rising, appears in Yeats's poetry both as a personal rival and as a figure transfigured by heroic sacrifice.

memes.label: John O'Leary
memes.description: John O'Leary (1830–1907), Fenian leader and Irish nationalist, was W. B. Yeats's most important early mentor — the man who taught him that "there are things a man must not do to save a nation" and who became, after his death, the emblem of a lost "Romantic Ireland."

memes.label: John Millington Synge
memes.description: John Millington Synge (1871–1909), playwright and co-director of the Abbey Theatre, was W. B. Yeats's close collaborator in the Irish dramatic movement — the "enquiring man" whose realistic portrayals of Irish life provoked both admiration and riot.

memes.label: Knocknarea
memes.description: Knocknarea, a mountain in Co. Sligo topped by the massive stone cairn of Queen Maeve, is one of Yeats's most potent landscape symbols — a nexus of Irish mythology, megalithic mystery, and feminine power.

memes.label: Lady Gregory
memes.description: Lady Gregory, Irish folklorist and playwright, was Yeats's most important patron and collaborator — co-founder of the Abbey Theatre and the anchor of his creative life at Coole Park for over thirty years.

memes.label: Lough Gill
memes.description: Lough Gill in Co. Sligo, the lake containing the island of Innisfree, is the central body of water in Yeats's Sligo landscape — a site of reverie, escape, and the pastoral imagination.

memes.label: Love in Yeats
memes.description: Love in Yeats's poetry is a transformative force that operates simultaneously as personal passion, mystical devotion, and national allegory, centering on his lifelong, unrequited love for Maud Gonne.

memes.label: Magic and the Occult
memes.description: Yeats's lifelong engagement with magical and occult traditions — the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, Rosicrucianism, spiritualism, and Celtic mysticism — formed the intellectual framework for his poetry and his philosophical system in A Vision.

memes.label: Mask Theory
memes.description: Yeats's doctrine of the Mask — the anti-self or opposite that the artist and individual must cultivate as a discipline of self-completion, central to his mature philosophy, poetics, and system of historical cycles.

memes.label: Maud Gonne
memes.description: Maud Gonne (1866–1953), Irish revolutionary and actress, was the great love and muse of Yeats's life — her presence pervades his poetry for over 50 years, from the Rose poems of the 1890s to the elegies of his final period.

memes.label: Meditations in Time of Civil War
aliases: Meditations in Time of Civil War (poem)

memes.label: Memory in Yeats
memes.description: In Yeats's poetry, memory is a creative and spiritual faculty — not passive recall but an active power that connects the living to ancestors, folklore, and the "Great Memory" of the Anima Mundi.

memes.label: Michael Robartes
memes.description: Fictional mystic and philosopher created by Yeats; a central persona in the poet's later work and the framing device for the esoteric system of A Vision.

memes.label: Moon Symbolism
memes.description: The moon in Yeats's system is the master symbol of cyclical change — its 28 phases in A Vision map the soul's journey through incarnation, the gyres of history, and the oscillation between objective and subjective being.

memes.label: Nature in Yeats
memes.description: Nature in Yeats's poetry is never mere scenery — it is a repository of spiritual meaning, a site of mythological encounter, and the ground of Irish identity, centered on the landscapes of Co. Sligo.

memes.label: Niamh
memes.description: Niamh (Niamh Chinn Óir, Niamh of the Golden Hair), daughter of the sea-god Manannán, is the fairy woman who carries Usheen away to the Land of Youth — Yeats's principal image of the supernatural feminine that calls the mortal to the otherworld.

memes.label: Oisin (Usheen)
memes.description: Legendary Irish poet-warrior of the Fenian Cycle, son of Finn mac Cumhaill, and protagonist of Yeats's longest early poem "The Wanderings of Usheen" (1889).

memes.label: Old Age in Yeats
memes.description: The treatment of old age — physical decay, sexual jealousy, wisdom, and creative fury — as a recurring theme in W. B. Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: Owen Aherne
memes.description: Owen Aherne is one of W. B. Yeats's fictional personae — a recurring character who, alongside Michael Robartes, appears in Yeats's poetry and prose as a vehicle for occult-philosophical dialogue, most notably in "The Phases of the Moon" and the framing apparatus of *A Vision*.

memes.label: Patrick Pearse
memes.description: Patrick Pearse (1879–1916), Irish teacher, poet, and revolutionary, was executed after the Easter Rising — Yeats commemorated him in 'Easter, 1916' with the famous line 'a terrible beauty is born.'

memes.label: Phases of the Moon
memes.description: The twenty-eight phases of the moon are the psychological core of Yeats's *A Vision* — a typology of human personality in which each phase corresponds to a particular ratio of the Four Faculties, forming a complete cycle of the soul's incarnations.

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: Plato is a foundational philosophical influence on W. B. Yeats, whose theory of Forms, doctrine of the Great Year, and vision of love as ascent shaped Yeats's own system of gyres, masks, and the soul's journey toward Unity of Being.

memes.label: Platonic Tradition
memes.description: The Platonic tradition — the lineage of idealist philosophy from Plato through Plotinus to Renaissance Neoplatonism — provided W. B. Yeats with the metaphysical framework for his symbolic system, his theory of eternal beauty, and his late reckoning with mortality.

memes.label: Plotinus
memes.description: Plotinus (c. 204–270 CE), the founding philosopher of Neoplatonism, was a central intellectual presence in W. B. Yeats's late poetry and prose — sometimes embraced as a guide to the eternal, sometimes rebelled against in the name of embodied life.

memes.label: Queen Maeve
memes.description: Queen Maeve (Medb) of Connacht, the warrior queen of Irish myth, fascinated Yeats as a figure of pre-Christian female sovereignty — sexual, martial, and autonomous.

memes.label: Rose (symbol)
memes.description: Central symbol in W. B. Yeats's poetry representing eternal beauty, mystical love, Ireland, and spiritual transcendence, drawing on Rosicrucian, Catholic, and Irish nationalist traditions.

memes.label: Sato's Sword
memes.description: Sato's sword is a symbolic object in W. B. Yeats's tower poems — a 550-year-old Japanese blade given to Yeats by Junzo Sato, representing changeless art and tradition in contrast to the flux of modern life.

relations.label: Meditations in Time of Civil War

memes.label: Shadow and Light
memes.description: In Yeats's symbolic system, shadow and light are the primordial antithetical pair — darkness is not merely the absence of light but a creative force, the Daimon or Mask that the soul pursues toward its completion.

memes.label: County Sligo
memes.description: County Sligo in the west of Ireland was Yeats's spiritual home — the landscape of his childhood summers with the Pollexfen family, and the imaginative ground of his poetry from the earliest work to his final resting place at Drumcliff.

memes.label: Song and Poetry
memes.description: In Yeats's work, song is the primordial form of poetry — an oral, musical tradition rooted in the Irish bardic past, where the poet is both singer and seer, keeper of ancestral memory and prophetic vision.

memes.label: Sorrow in Yeats
memes.description: The role of sorrow as a central emotional register and philosophical category in W. B. Yeats's poetry.

memes.label: Soul in Yeats
memes.description: In Yeats's poetic and philosophical system, the soul is an immortal essence that pursues its antithetical opposite across successive incarnations — a concept drawn from Neoplatonism, Irish fairy-lore, and Yeats's own occult cosmology.

memes.label: Spiritus Mundi
memes.description: The Spiritus Mundi is Yeats's term for a "general storehouse of images" — a collective reservoir of symbols and forms that have ceased to belong to any individual personality, from which dream images and poetic visions arise.

memes.label: Stone Imagery
memes.description: Stone in Yeats's poetry is the element of permanence, silence, and monumentality — the "cold stone" that resists time, the tower that stands against the flux of generation, the sculpted marble of Byzantine artifice.

memes.label: Swan in Yeats
memes.description: The swan as a multivalent symbol in Yeats' poetry, spanning mythological violence in "Leda and the Swan," elegiac beauty in "The Wild Swans at Coole," and the soul's solitary flight.

memes.label: The Anti-Self
memes.description: In Yeats's doctrine of the Mask, the anti-self is the deliberately cultivated opposite of one's natural character — "the most unlike, being my anti-self" — the image one strives to become through a lifetime of imaginative discipline.

memes.label: The Countess Cathleen
memes.description: The Countess Cathleen and Various Legends and Lyrics (1892) is W. B. Yeats's second book of poetry — a volume anchored by the title verse-drama in which an Irish countess sells her soul to feed the starving poor, drawing on Irish folklore and the Faust legend.
aliases: The Countess Cathleen and Various Legends and Lyrics

memes.label: The Double Vision of Michael Robartes
memes.description: The Double Vision of Michael Robartes is a poem by W. B. Yeats first published in The Wild Swans at Coole (1919), depicting a mystical vision on the Rock of Cashel that synthesizes Eastern and Western spiritual symbols.

memes.label: The Fisherman
memes.description: The Fisherman is W. B. Yeats's imagined ideal reader — a "wise and simple man" from Connemara, created in scorn of the actual Irish reading public, who represents the poet's longing for an audience worthy of his art.

memes.label: The Rose (collection)
memes.description: The Rose (1893), Yeats's second poetry collection and the title section of his early work, introduced the Rose as his central symbol of eternal beauty, mystical love, and Ireland — fusing Rosicrucian mysticism with Irish nationalism.

memes.label: The Sidhe
memes.description: The Sidhe are the faery folk of Irish mythology, the gods of ancient Ireland who still ride the countryside, closely associated with wind, hills, and the otherworld.

memes.label: The Song of Wandering Aengus
memes.description: The Song of Wandering Aengus is an early poem by W. B. Yeats from The Wind Among the Reeds (1899), retelling an Irish myth of the love-god Aengus's visionary quest for a supernatural beloved.

memes.label: The Tower (collection)
memes.description: The Tower (1928), named for Thoor Ballylee, is Yeats's greatest single volume — containing 'Sailing to Byzantium,' 'Leda and the Swan,' 'Among School Children,' and the title poem that declares 'this tower is my symbol.'

memes.label: The Tower in Yeats
memes.description: The tower as the dominant architectural symbol of Yeats's later poetry — at once a literal dwelling (Thoor Ballylee), an emblem of solitary wisdom, a figure for historical continuity and ancestral violence, and a mandala of the soul's vertical ascent through the winding stair.

memes.label: The Wanderings of Usheen
memes.description: The Wanderings of Usheen (1889), Yeats's first major narrative poem, is a dialogue between the aged pagan hero Usheen (Oisín) and St. Patrick — a lament for the passing of pagan Ireland and the greatest of Yeats's early long poems.

memes.label: The Wild Swans at Coole (collection)
memes.description: The Wild Swans at Coole (1919) marks the transition to Yeats's mature style — the title poem's counted swans and autumn beauty announce the elegiac, self-scrutinizing voice of middle Yeats.

memes.label: The Wind Among the Reeds
memes.description: The Wind Among the Reeds (1899), one of Yeats's most celebrated early collections, contains poems of the Sidhe, occult love, and the secret Rose — the culmination of his Celtic twilight period.

memes.label: Thoor Ballylee
memes.description: Thoor Ballylee, a 15th-century Norman tower in Co. Galway purchased by Yeats in 1917, became his summer home and the presiding symbol of his late poetry — a stone monument connecting him to Irish history and the Anglo-Irish tradition.

memes.label: Time and Eternity
memes.description: The central Yeatsian tension between mortal time and the artifice of eternity, expressed through symbols of the golden bird, Byzantium, and the gyres of history.

memes.label: Tír na nÓg
memes.description: The Land of Youth in Irish mythology — the Otherworld realm to which the fairy woman Niamh carries Oisin in Yeats's narrative poem "The Wanderings of Usheen".

memes.label: Tree Imagery in Yeats
memes.description: Trees and woodland as a symbolic system in Yeats' poetry — the hazel of Celtic wisdom, the apple of faery, the oak of endurance, and the sacred tree linking earth to heaven.

memes.label: Tuatha Dé Danann
memes.description: The Tuatha Dé Danann — the Tribes of the goddess Danu — are the ancient gods of Ireland who, in W. B. Yeats's mythological system, became the Sidhe or fairy folk of Irish folk belief, representing powers of light, fruitfulness, and visionary imagination against the ancestral darkness.

memes.label: Water Imagery
memes.description: Water in Yeats's symbolic system is the "signature of the fruitfulness of the body" — the element of generation, desire, the unconscious, and the maternal, appearing throughout his poetry in lakes, seas, wells, and fountains.

memes.label: William Blake
memes.description: William Blake (1757–1827), poet, artist, and visionary, was the most important literary influence on Yeats's early thought — his system of contraries, symbolic imagination, and prophetic voice shaped Yeats's own poetic and philosophical development.

memes.label: The Winding Stair
memes.description: The winding stair is the spiral staircase of Yeats's tower at Thoor Ballylee — a physical structure that becomes a central poetic symbol of the soul's ascent, the cyclical movement of consciousness, and the gyre made architecture.

memes.label: Wisdom in Yeats
memes.description: In Yeats's poetry, wisdom is not rational knowledge but an ancient, intuitive understanding — "dreaming wisdom" inherited from the Druids, carried in folk memory, and accessible through poetic vision rather than intellect.
